channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: Blighty newsletter: Britainâs most popular names
item.description: Parentsâ taste in baby names is changing, writes Hugo Gye, our British political correspondent

item.title: State funding threatens Chinese tech
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: When Chinaâs open-source AI is a trap
item.description: Americaâs quest for AI dominance is scary. China is not the solution

item.title: Cities are rethinking what happens after dark
item.description: Making it easier to party is becoming serious public policy

item.title: Trumpâs Hormuz brinkmanship is worsening a global fuel crunch
item.description: Rising oil prices are only part of the problem

item.title: Britainâs bookies have weathered a tricky World Cup
item.description: But bigger problems are stacking up

item.title: What investment gurus get wrong
item.description: To see a countryâs financial follies, look to its celebrity advisers

item.title: Haunted houses are in demand in Japan
item.description: Whatâs behind the growing desire for once-shunned âincident propertiesâ?

item.title: Inside the worldâs most important exam (part one)
item.description: Our weekly podcast on China. This week, what is it like to prepare for the gaokao?

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: Another looming atrocity in Sudan
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, argues that the conflict is not âforgottenâ but âneglectedâ

item.title: Donald Trump has no good options for reopening the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Yet the stand-off has costs for cash-strapped Iran, too

item.title: The case of the missing totem: Aung San Suu Kyi
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: Turkeyâs plan to benefit from the Iran war and pinning down the ideal amount of sleep

item.title: How to defeat dementia
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: Demis Hassabis has a plan to harness AI safely
item.description: The Google DeepMind co-founder sets out his vision in an exclusive interview

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Ukraineâs Game of Drones
item.description: Oliver Carroll, our Ukraine correspondent, assesses the performance of the countryâs defence ministerÂ

item.title: The stubborn scarcity of female breadwinners
item.description: Wives who outearn their husbands remain rare in America

item.title: How high can Red Bull fly?
item.description: The worldâs most formidable marketing machine faces growing threats

item.title: Londonâs private membersâ clubs are proliferating
item.description: But building a new community is harder than it looks

item.title: Ukraineâs reforming defence minister is under fire
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov has been trying to reform the army. The generals are pushing back

item.title: A squeezed China is trying to wring more from its state assets
item.description: Its latest campaign wonât solve its debt woes, but inches in the right direction

item.title: Is Aung San Suu Kyi dead?
item.description: Myanmarâs jailed leader has not been seen since 2022

item.title: Americaâs other elections problem
item.description: No other rich democracy hands as many decisions directly to its voters

item.title: A hawk who flew on political winds: Lindsey Graham
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: a glimpse into AI frontier modelsâ values and new enthusiasm for the Old Masters

item.title: An oligarch speaks out
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: Lindsey Graham represented the arc of his party
item.description: South Carolinaâs senior senator is dead at 71

item.title: An incomplete list of falling objects in India
item.description: Prosperity and infrastructure development have not come with improved safety

item.title: The Ebola epidemic is getting out of control
item.description: It has spread to five provinces in Congo and may soon reach South Sudan

item.title: Storm clouds gather over Americaâs financial supremacy
item.description: Its payments firms may be the first casualties

item.title: China is dealing with its own manosphere
item.description: But online misogyny is only one corner of a much bigger problem

item.title: Elon Musk is building a form of capitalism that Adam Smith would hate
item.description: The merchants are becoming princes, writes Tim OâReilly

item.title: Plot Twist newsletter: What will the new generation of zillionaires collect?
item.description: Alexandra Suich Bass, our culture editor, examines whether the uber-rich pursue Old Master paintings

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The weirdness of American socialism
item.description: John Prideaux, our executive editor, on the rise of a new political class from the activist left

item.title: The new homeschoolers
item.description: Could the global rise in homeschooling be bad for society?

item.title: How to train for a heatwave
item.description: A nice, hot bath can go a long way

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Â The man who would change Russia
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: How Donald Trump makes money
item.description: Our weekly podcast on America. This week, the presidentâs extraordinary financial windfall

item.title: What to watch this week
item.description: Our choice of best, and worst, in film and television

item.title: The Economist is hiring freelance video editors
item.description: Support our award-winning editorial video department

item.title: The wild, gripping story of the Nord Stream pipeline bombing
item.description: A technicolour account of the sabotage suggests how fast the world has changed

item.title: Where is the home of football?
item.description: England invented the sport. But its modern centre of gravity lies elsewhere

item.title: Putinâs options: an oligarch speaks out
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: Kyiv under siege and we reach the end of Route 66Â

item.title: The Economistâs long struggle with the oil price
item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, why we woz wrong

item.title: Mona Khalil defied danger to protect her âfamilyâ
item.description: Lebanonâs most dedicated conservationist was killed on June 19th, aged 76

item.title: The brightest tourism trend can be found in the darkest places
item.description: Stargazing is an illuminating business

item.title: Economic data, commodities and markets
The real value of the Bayeux tapestry
item.description: The precious work, insured for $1bn, is bound for Britain. Itâs more famous there than in France

item.title: The history of the human face is more than skin deep
item.description: An exuberant new book includes physiognomy, portraiture and plastic surgery

item.title: The secret to good questions
item.description: Consideration, consistency and curveballs

item.title: Could a Chinese bike win the Tour de France?
item.description: The countryâs manufacturers hope that high-performance models will improve their image

item.title: Chinaâs media gloat at Britainâs chaotic democracy
item.description: But Chinese viewers also love British comedy that laughs at politics

item.title: Child suspects are being prosecuted at younger ages
item.description: Some governments are lowering the age of criminal responsibility. That may backfire

item.title: China releases a prominent Christian pastor
item.description: Jin Mingri formerly ran a large house church in Beijing

item.title: How South Korean Buddhists are trying to stay relevant
item.description: Life is suffering; the Buddha can help

item.title: Laos has big dreams of exporting energy
item.description: But its dams have flooded it with debt and other problems

item.title: Americans and Canadians are squabbling over mines
item.description: The frontier complicates disagreements between indigenous groups

item.title: Ahmed al-Sharaa is quashing Syriaâs vast captagon trade
item.description: But pills that cost cents to produce still sell for $25

item.title: The leftâs common-sense problem on crime
item.description: Radical ideas may seriously harm the Democratic Party

item.title: Can Africa take the Asian path to growth?
item.description: Stalling agricultural productivity makes it look unlikely

item.title: Texas is waging a battle to stop âIslamificationâ
item.description: Americaâs biggest Republican state offers a playbook for a new identity politics

item.title: Why Donald Trump wonât let a doomed bill die
item.description: The SAVE Act is unlikely to pass Congress. Donald Trump may prefer it that way

item.title: Rise of the Gen-Z Luddite
item.description: Resistance to tech is brewing among digital natives

item.title: Europeâs public broadcasters go from prime time to hard-to-find
item.description: Apathetic teenagers and culture warriors are hobbling venerable state media outlets

item.title: Poland and Ukraine have fallen out over history
item.description: The row could have lasting consequences for Europeâs security

item.title: Some Scottish wrens are a striking case of âisland syndromeâ
item.description: They have got much bigger than their mainland cousins

item.title: Is AI writing taking over Westminster?
item.description: Several influential policy papers are failing AI detectors

item.title: Theresa May: Britainâs firstâand lastâwoke prime ministerÂ
item.description: That whooshing sound was the Overton window hurtling byÂ

item.title: Nigel Farageâs stunts made him a successful politician. They wonât make him PM
item.description: The theatrics could point to Reform UKâs weaknesses

item.title: Britainâs social housing subsidises the wrong people
item.description: And is bad for growth

item.title: How realistic is Andy Burnhamâs council-house building pledge?
item.description: It would be far better if Britainâs prime-minister-in-waiting followed âManchesterismâ

item.title: How dementia is being defeated
item.description: Incidence among the elderly is falling fast

item.title: Why are big AI labs hiring so many philosophers?
item.description: Also this week, the impact of the Iran war, academic standards, tall men, Alan Greenspan, Warrington

item.title: Who is capable of evil?
item.description: Stop lowering the age of criminal responsibility

item.title: A no-brainer for protecting your brain
item.description: One simple vaccination may dramatically reduce the risk of dementia

item.title: England needs fewer council homes, not more
item.description: Andy Burnhamâs plan is no way to ease the housing crisis

item.title: Two cheers for Trump Accounts
item.description: The grubby scheme contains the seeds of a good idea

item.title: Cartoon: The real threat to global growth
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Business
Politics
Indian mangoes have been banned from Japan
item.description: Itâs the latest sign of a widespread food-safety problem

item.description: Also on the daily podcast: mandatory filial piety and our Route 66 trip reaches Oklahoma

item.title: China may struggle to fund Xi Jinpingâs tech dreams
item.description: Even though capital markets are staging a comeback

item.title: An ageing society might not cost too much
item.description: A dystopian vision of the future may not come to pass

item.title: A top Russian oligarch breaks the silence
item.description: An interview with Andrey Melnichenko on the future of his country

item.title: The man who would change Russia
item.description: A leading oligarch speaks out, warning of the looming disaster facing his country

item.title: Why a broken Russia is bad for the world
item.description: When the war in Ukraine ends, Moscow will need to be part of the new security architecture, argues Andrey Melnichenko

item.title: NATO survives its bipolar summit
item.description: Allies breath a sigh of relief; Ukraine may even cheer

item.title: Elon Musk and the age of the corporate leviathan
item.description: For the worldâs largest companies, the normal rules of corporate governance no longer apply

item.title: Is Donald Trump serious in declaring the ceasefire with Iran over?
item.description: Iranâs ultra-nationalist new leaders may welcome his bluster

item.title: Microsoftâs gaming strategy has misfired badly
item.description: A supply-chain crisis for Xbox couldnât have come at a worse time

item.title: How the biggest trees survive droughts
item.description: Being tall would be a disadvantage, if not for some clever engineering

item.title: How to hide from killer drones
item.description: In the Ukraine war, anti-AI tactics are producing bizarre forms of camouflage

item.title: A statisticianâs guide to Wimbledon
item.description: How to turn a tiny advantage into sporting glory

item.title: The future of chipmaking looks more like Manhattan than Silicon Valley
item.description: Constrained by physics and politics, chipmakers are building upwards

item.title: Russians are growing anxious and angry
item.description: The war has come home and is everyoneâs problem

item.title: Prepare for a world of ad hoc coalitions
item.description: Neither wishful multilateralism nor cold realpolitik will do. Mark Carney and Alexander Stubb suggest a third way

item.title: Brazilian cities are building vast surveillance networks
item.description: Can they combat the countryâs biggest security threats?

item.title: A new era of biology beckons
item.description: Our podcast on science and technology. Edith Heard, the boss of the Francis Crick Institute in London, on pushing the frontier of science

item.title: Three ways the LNG market could crack before winter
item.description: War, weather and outages may still send gas prices soaring

item.title: Marine warfare: Le Pen runs for presidentÂ
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: whale sanctuaries and Route 66âs commercial past

item.description: The point of the mega-funeral is to demonstrate the power of the regime, writes Anton La Guardia, our diplomatic editor

item.title: Marine Le Pen is running for the French presidency
item.description: Despite being found guilty by the Paris court of appeal

item.title: AI has taken over the stock market. The bond market is next
item.description: Judging credit risk of the AI boom is difficultÂ

item.title: Europeâs economy is a mess. Its stock markets are a steal
item.description: International investors still arenât interested

item.title: What Graham Platnerâs collapse means for the Democrats
item.description: The troubled Senate nominee has suspended his campaign in Maine

item.title: Nigel Farageâs rather silly by-election
item.description: He hopes to distract from his finances. The joke may be on him

item.description: Our weekly podcast on China. This week, meet the countryâs beachgoers

item.description: But it is proving easier to design chips than to make them

item.description: The character of Englandâs footballers reflects the state of the nation, writes Andy Miller, our special correspondent

item.title: Why Old Master paintings are back in vogue
item.description: Attitudes towards art, value and what it takes to be a good collector are changing

item.title: Continental rift: NATOâs tense summit
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: vibe lawyering and day two of our Route 66 roadtrip

item.title: Medicine can treat obesity, but canât agree on what obesity is
item.description: Labelling it a disease means millions will get treatment they may not need, writes Francesco Rubino

item.description: Visions of a high-tech hub in Hokkaido hinge on a single government-backed firm

item.title: If ludicrous Trump flattery can save NATO, bring it on
item.description: The NATO chief Mark Rutte is quite annoying. It is in a worthy cause

item.description: The rise and fall of the institutionâs love for green growth

item.description: Supersize capital flows are reshaping businessâand building risk

item.description: Can it keep its plans on track?

item.description: China moves a step closer to having an operational ânuclear triadâ

item.description: See how 173 cities compare in EIUâs annual ranking

item.description: An old exercise method is newly trendy

item.description: Donald Trumpâs legacy is still unfolding. âIn the end he may be best known for reaping riches from the office only 45 men have had the honour to hold,â writes Charlotte Howard, our US editor

item.description: Most Europeans want stronger armed forces, but are unwilling to pay for them

item.description: Credit greater accuracy and better communication

item.description: Defence is being re-energised. Now industry must produce more, better and faster, write Ursula von der Leyen and Mark RutteÂ

item.description: It reveals how much it has already changed

item.description: Even tiny bursts of activity have measurable benefits

item.description: Tales of resilience, from the Founding Fathers to fine dining

A23a had more adventures than most

item.description: We used artificial intelligence to test the accuracy of our forecasts

item.description: The technology giant he built is being yanked around by the state

item.description: You could read Homer. You could see the film. Or you could just go to work

item.description: German tanks are returning to a region they once razed

item.description: It uses them more intensively than anywhere in the world

item.description: But only if they actually go ahead

item.description: Turkey and Israel are natural allies and yet they are increasingly at odds

item.description: A light aircraft flew into Beijingâs tallest building. A news blackout followed

item.description: The continentâs border-control system is out of control

item.description: They make the universeâs most extreme gravitational laboratories

item.title: Why Cambridge and Cambridge need each other
item.description: Two of the worldâs most innovative cities have the same name and completely opposite problems. That is leading to closer collaboration

item.description: Ahead of its 250th birthday, an empirical look shows the worldâs leading country no longer stands so far apart
